background_cases: A bag containing three apples and a bag containing three oranges have the same number of items.
Two flocks of sheep can be compared by pairing each sheep in one flock with a sheep in the other.
The fingers on one hand and the toes on one foot are equal in number, as shown by one-to-one correspondence.
definition: Two collections have the same size if and only if (i) there is a one-to-one correspondence between them, (ii) neither collection has elements left over, and (iii) a collection is always larger than any of its proper parts.
conditions: one-to-one correspondence exists
no elements left over
whole larger than proper part
counterexample: The natural numbers and the even numbers can be placed in one-to-one correspondence (n maps to 2n), with no elements left over. Yet the even numbers are a proper part of the natural numbers. The third condition — that the whole is always larger than its proper part — fails for infinite collections.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved finite collections, for which the principle that the whole is greater than the part is trivially true. The definition imported this finite intuition into a context where it breaks down — the infinite.
passage:
Consider the following comparisons. A bag containing three apples and a bag containing three oranges have the same number of items; we verify this by pairing each apple with an orange. Two flocks of sheep can be compared by matching each sheep in one flock with a sheep in the other. The fingers on one hand and the toes on one foot are equal in number, as shown by one-to-one correspondence. From these cases one abstracts a definition: two collections have the same size if and only if there is a one-to-one correspondence between them, neither has elements left over, and a collection is always larger than any of its proper parts.

Yet the third condition, so natural for finite collections, fails spectacularly in the infinite case. Consider the natural numbers and the even numbers. The function that maps each natural number n to the even number 2n establishes a perfect one-to-one correspondence; no element is left over on either side. The first two conditions are met. But the even numbers are a proper part of the natural numbers — every even number is a natural number, but not conversely. The third condition asserts that the whole must be larger than its proper part, yet the correspondence shows them to be the same size. The background cases were all finite, and the principle that the whole exceeds the part holds for finite collections. The definition failed to notice that this principle is a feature of finitude, not of size as such.

background_cases: Whether a given natural number is even can be decided by checking its last digit.
Whether a given propositional formula is a tautology can be decided by truth tables.
Whether a given integer is divisible by seven can be decided by performing the division.
definition: A mathematical question is decidable if and only if (i) it can be formulated precisely, (ii) there exists a method that will in principle answer it, and (iii) the method can be carried out in a finite number of steps.
conditions: can be formulated precisely
method exists to answer it
method terminates in finite steps
counterexample: The question 'Does this Turing machine halt on empty input?' can be formulated with perfect precision, yet no method exists that will answer it in general — the halting problem is undecidable. The first condition is met, but the second and third fail.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved simple mathematical properties for which decision procedures exist. The definition assumed that precise formulation guarantees the existence of a decision method, but the existence of undecidable problems shows that formalisability and decidability come apart.
passage:
Consider three cases in which a mathematical question is readily decided. Whether a natural number is even can be determined by inspecting its last digit. Whether a propositional formula is a tautology can be settled by constructing a truth table. Whether an integer is divisible by seven can be resolved by performing the division. In each case, the question is precisely formulated, a method exists to answer it, and the method terminates in finitely many steps. One might therefore define decidability: a mathematical question is decidable if and only if it can be formulated precisely, there exists a method that will in principle answer it, and the method terminates in a finite number of steps.

But consider the question: does this Turing machine, started on empty tape, eventually halt? The question can be formulated with perfect precision — there is no ambiguity about what a Turing machine is or what halting means. The first condition is met. Yet no method exists that will answer this question for every Turing machine. The halting problem, as it is called, is undecidable: no algorithm can determine, for an arbitrary machine, whether it halts. The background cases were all questions about simple mathematical properties — parity, tautologyhood, divisibility — for which decision procedures happen to exist. The definition assumed that precise formulation suffices for decidability, but the theory of computation has shown that these come apart: some perfectly precise questions have no general decision procedure.

background_cases: The real numbers form a complete ordered field: every non-empty bounded set has a least upper bound.
The closed interval [0, 1] is complete as a metric space: every Cauchy sequence converges within it.
The p-adic numbers are complete with respect to the p-adic metric: every Cauchy sequence has a limit.
definition: A number system is complete if and only if (i) it is equipped with a notion of distance or size, (ii) sequences whose terms grow arbitrarily close together have limits within the system, and (iii) the arithmetic operations are continuous with respect to the notion of distance.
conditions: equipped with notion of distance
Cauchy sequences converge within system
arithmetic continuous with respect to distance
counterexample: Consider the rational numbers with the usual metric. They are equipped with a notion of distance (the absolute value of the difference). The arithmetic operations — addition, multiplication, and their inverses — are continuous with respect to this distance. The first and third conditions are met. Yet there exist Cauchy sequences of rationals — for instance, the decimal approximations of the square root of two — whose terms grow arbitrarily close together but which have no limit in the rationals. The second condition fails, and the system is incomplete.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all systems in which completeness was already established by construction or by theorem. The definition captured the features these complete systems share but did not exclude systems that possess some of those features — a metric and continuous arithmetic — without possessing completeness itself.
passage:
Consider three complete number systems. The real numbers form a complete ordered field: every non-empty set bounded above has a least upper bound, and every Cauchy sequence converges. The closed interval from zero to one is a complete metric space in its own right. And the p-adic numbers, equipped with a different metric, are complete with respect to that metric. From these cases one might define completeness: a number system is complete if and only if it is equipped with a notion of distance, sequences whose terms grow arbitrarily close together have limits within the system, and the arithmetic operations are continuous with respect to the distance.

Yet the rational numbers, equipped with the usual absolute-value metric, satisfy the first and third conditions whilst failing the second. The rationals possess a perfectly good notion of distance, and the arithmetic operations — addition, subtraction, multiplication, division — are all continuous with respect to this distance. But the sequence of decimal approximations to the square root of two — 1, 1.4, 1.41, 1.414, and so on — is a Cauchy sequence whose terms grow arbitrarily close together, yet which has no limit among the rationals. The system is incomplete despite possessing a metric and continuous arithmetic. The background cases were systems in which completeness had already been secured, and the definition abstracted features common to those systems without recognising that a metric and continuous operations are necessary but not sufficient for completeness.

background_cases: The natural numbers 1, 2, 3, ... form an infinite set: one can always find another number beyond any given finite collection.
The integers ..., -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, ... form an infinite set: they extend without bound in both directions.
The rational numbers between zero and one form an infinite set: between any two rationals there is always a third.
definition: A set is infinite if and only if (i) it is non-empty, (ii) it cannot be exhausted by any finite list, and (iii) it can be put into one-to-one correspondence with a proper part of itself.
conditions: non-empty
cannot be exhausted by finite list
in bijection with a proper subset of itself
counterexample: Each of the background cases involves a countably infinite set — one that can be listed as a sequence, even if the list is unending. The natural numbers, the integers, and the rationals between zero and one are all countably infinite, and each can be placed in one-to-one correspondence with a proper part of itself. But consider the set of all subsets of the natural numbers — the power set. It satisfies all three conditions: it is non-empty, it cannot be exhausted by any finite list, and it can be paired with a proper part of itself. Yet it is of a fundamentally larger cardinality than any of the background cases. The definition, though correct, makes no distinction between the countably and uncountably infinite.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all countably infinite sets. The definition captured what they shared — inexhaustibility and self-embeddability — but because all three examples had the same cardinality, the definition failed to register the possibility of distinct magnitudes of infinity.
passage:
Consider three cases of infinite sets. The natural numbers form an infinite set, for one can always find another number beyond any given finite collection. The integers extend without bound in both directions, and no finite list exhausts them. The rational numbers between zero and one form an infinite set as well, for between any two rationals there is always a third. From these cases one might define infinity: a set is infinite if and only if it is non-empty, it cannot be exhausted by any finite list, and it can be placed in one-to-one correspondence with a proper part of itself.

This definition is, so far as it goes, correct. Each of the background cases satisfies all three conditions: the natural numbers can be paired with the even numbers, the integers with the positive integers, and so on. But now consider the set of all subsets of the natural numbers — the power set. It too is non-empty, it cannot be finitely listed, and it can be put into correspondence with a proper part of itself. The three conditions are met. Yet this set is of a fundamentally different character from those in our background cases: Cantor demonstrated that it cannot be placed in one-to-one correspondence with the natural numbers. It is uncountably infinite — a larger infinity. The background cases, all countably infinite, misled us into a definition that, whilst correct as far as it goes, fails to register the profoundly important distinction between different magnitudes of infinity.

background_cases: A line is one-dimensional: a single coordinate suffices to specify any point upon it.
A plane is two-dimensional: two coordinates are required to specify a point.
Ordinary space is three-dimensional: three coordinates are needed.
definition: The dimension of a space is the number of independent coordinates required to specify a point within it, such that (i) the number is a non-negative integer, (ii) it equals the minimum number of real-valued parameters needed, and (iii) it is invariant under continuous deformation of the space.
conditions: dimension is non-negative integer
equals minimum number of real parameters
invariant under continuous deformation
counterexample: Consider the Sierpinski triangle, a fractal constructed by iteratively removing central triangles from an equilateral triangle. It is embedded in the plane, yet it is 'thinner' than the plane — it has Lebesgue measure zero. A single real parameter can trace a continuous path through it, yet it is not one-dimensional in the intuitive sense: it branches and ramifies at every scale. Its Hausdorff dimension is log 3 / log 2, approximately 1.585 — not a non-negative integer. The first condition fails. The concept of dimension, it appears, is not confined to integer values.
abductive_insight: The background cases — lines, planes, ordinary space — were all smooth, homogeneous objects whose dimensions are whole numbers. The definition captured the integer-valued character of dimension in these regular settings but could not accommodate fractal objects whose dimensions are non-integral.
passage:
Consider three familiar cases. A line is one-dimensional: a single coordinate specifies any point upon it. A plane requires two coordinates; ordinary space requires three. From these cases one might define dimension: the dimension of a space is the number of independent coordinates needed to specify a point, this number being a non-negative integer, equal to the minimum number of real-valued parameters required, and invariant under continuous deformation.

Yet this definition, drawn from smooth and regular spaces, encounters objects it cannot accommodate. Consider the Sierpinski triangle, a figure constructed by iteratively removing central triangles from an equilateral triangle, ad infinitum. The resulting set is embedded in the plane but has Lebesgue measure zero — it is, in a precise sense, thinner than any two-dimensional region. A continuous curve can be drawn through it, suggesting one-dimensionality, but the set branches and ramifies at every scale in a manner quite unlike any curve. Its Hausdorff dimension — a generalisation of the familiar notion — is log three divided by log two, approximately 1.585. This is not a non-negative integer. The first condition of our definition fails. The background cases were all smooth, homogeneous objects whose dimensions happened to be whole numbers. The definition captured the integer character of dimension in those well-behaved settings but could not anticipate objects whose geometry is so irregular that their dimension falls between the integers.

background_cases: The length of the interval [0, 1] is 1; the length of [0, 2] is 2. Length is additive: the length of two disjoint intervals is the sum of their lengths.
The area of a unit square is 1; the area of a rectangle is the product of its sides. Area is additive for disjoint regions.
The volume of a unit cube is 1; volumes add for disjoint solids.
definition: The measure of a region is a non-negative real number such that (i) the measure of a basic region (interval, rectangle, box) equals its geometric size, (ii) the measure of a union of finitely many disjoint regions equals the sum of their measures, and (iii) every region has a definite measure.
conditions: basic regions have geometric size as measure
finite additivity for disjoint regions
every region has definite measure
counterexample: Consider a sequence of disjoint intervals whose lengths are 1/2, 1/4, 1/8, and so on, ad infinitum. The sum of their lengths is 1. The finite additivity condition handles any finite sub-collection, but the full infinite collection requires countable additivity — the extension of the additivity principle to countably many disjoint sets. Furthermore, Vitali's construction produces a set of real numbers that cannot be assigned any measure at all, whether by finite or countable additivity, without contradicting the requirement that intervals have their geometric length. The third condition — that every region has a measure — fails.
abductive_insight: The background cases involved finite unions of simple geometric objects. The definition abstracted finite additivity, which sufficed for those cases, but the passage to infinitely many disjoint sets requires countable additivity, and even this stronger condition cannot assign measures to all sets of real numbers.
passage:
Consider the following cases of measurement. The length of the interval from zero to one is one; the length of the interval from zero to two is two; and the length of two disjoint intervals is the sum of their individual lengths. The area of a unit square is one, and areas add for disjoint regions. The volume of a unit cube is one, and volumes add for disjoint solids. From these cases one might extract a definition: the measure of a region is a non-negative real number such that basic regions have their geometric size as measure, the measure of a union of finitely many disjoint regions equals the sum of their measures, and every region has a definite measure.

But this definition encounters two difficulties when we move beyond its finite origins. First, consider a sequence of disjoint intervals of lengths one-half, one-quarter, one-eighth, and so on. The sum of their lengths is one, and we should wish to say that their union has measure one. But the definition guarantees additivity only for finite collections; the infinite case requires a stronger principle — countable additivity — which the background cases did not demand. Second, and more gravely, the construction of Vitali demonstrates the existence of a set of real numbers to which no non-negative real number can be assigned as measure without violating the additivity condition for intervals. The third condition — that every region has a definite measure — is therefore untenable. The background cases, being finite unions of simple geometric objects, concealed both limitations: the need for countable additivity and the inevitable existence of non-measurable sets.

background_cases: The natural numbers under their usual ordering are well-ordered: every non-empty subset has a least element.
The set {1, 2, 3, ..., 100} is well-ordered: any non-empty subset, being finite, has a minimum.
The ordinal numbers up to omega are well-ordered: every non-empty collection of them has a smallest member.
definition: A linearly ordered set is well-ordered if and only if (i) every pair of elements is comparable, (ii) the ordering is transitive, and (iii) there are no infinite strictly descending sequences.
conditions: every pair comparable
ordering transitive
no infinite descending sequences
counterexample: Consider the ordinal omega plus omega — the natural numbers followed by another copy of the natural numbers. Under the usual ordinal ordering, every pair is comparable, the ordering is transitive, and there are no infinite strictly descending sequences (one cannot descend infinitely within either copy, and one cannot cross from the second copy back to the first). The three conditions are met, and the set is well-ordered. Now consider the rational numbers between zero and one. Every pair is comparable, the ordering is transitive, and — here is the critical point — there do exist infinite descending sequences, for instance 1/2, 1/3, 1/4, 1/5, ... The third condition correctly identifies this set as not well-ordered. But the prohibition on infinite descending sequences, though equivalent to well-ordering given the axiom of choice, is not constructively equivalent; in the absence of choice, a set may have no infinite descending sequence yet fail to have a least element in every non-empty subset.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all sets in which well-ordering was patent and the equivalence between 'no infinite descent' and 'every subset has a minimum' was unproblematic. The definition captured the no-descent characterisation, which is equivalent in classical mathematics but diverges from the least-element characterisation in constructive or choice-free settings.
passage:
Consider three well-ordered sets. The natural numbers under their usual ordering are well-ordered: every non-empty subset has a least element. The finite set consisting of the integers from one to one hundred is well-ordered for the same reason. And the ordinal numbers up to omega — the first infinite ordinal — are well-ordered. From these cases one might characterise well-ordering: a linearly ordered set is well-ordered if and only if every pair of elements is comparable, the ordering is transitive, and there are no infinite strictly descending sequences.

The third condition — the prohibition of infinite descent — is the distinctive one, and in the presence of the axiom of choice it is equivalent to the requirement that every non-empty subset has a least element. But this equivalence is not absolute. In constructive mathematics, or in set-theoretic frameworks that do not assume the axiom of choice, a linearly ordered set may harbour no infinite descending sequence and yet possess a non-empty subset with no least element. The two characterisations come apart. The background cases were all sets in which the equivalence held unproblematically — they were either finite or built from the natural numbers, where the well-ordering principle is evident. The definition abstracted the no-descent condition, which happened to coincide with the least-element condition in every background case, but which in more general settings may fail to capture the full strength of well-ordering.

background_cases: The natural number 5 counts a collection of five objects; it is positive, discrete, and can be placed on the number line.
The integer -3 represents a deficit or a position to the left of zero on the number line; it is discrete and ordered.
The rational number 3/4 represents a proportion; it can be expressed as a ratio of integers and placed on the number line.
definition: An entity is a number if and only if (i) it can be ordered on a single line from least to greatest, (ii) it arises from counting or from the division of one integer by another, and (iii) it can be compared with any other number by the relation 'less than.'
conditions: ordered on single line from least to greatest
arises from counting or integer division
comparable with any other number by less-than
counterexample: The complex number 2 + 3i arises not from counting or division but from the extension of the number system to include a square root of -1. Complex numbers cannot be ordered on a single line: there is no consistent way to say that 2 + 3i is less than or greater than 1 + 4i while preserving the algebraic properties of an ordered field. The first and third conditions fail, yet complex numbers are indispensable in mathematics and physics.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all real numbers — natural, integer, or rational — that admit of a linear ordering. The definition abstracted linear orderability as essential, but the complex numbers form a number system in which no compatible total order exists, demonstrating that the concept of number extends beyond the linearly ordered.
passage:
Consider three numbers. The natural number 5 counts a collection. The integer -3 represents a deficit. The rational 3/4 represents a proportion. Each can be placed on the number line and compared with any other by the relation 'less than.' From these cases one might hold that an entity is a number if and only if it can be ordered on a single line from least to greatest, it arises from counting or from division of integers, and it can be compared with any other number by the less-than relation.

But this definition, drawn from the real numbers, proves too restrictive. Consider the complex number 2 + 3i. It does not arise from counting or from the division of integers; it arises from the algebraic extension of the reals to include a root of x^2 + 1 = 0. And the complex numbers cannot be ordered on a single line in a way that is compatible with their algebraic structure: any total ordering of the complex field would violate the property that positive squares are positive. There is no consistent sense in which 2 + 3i is less than or greater than 1 + 4i. The first and third conditions fail. Yet complex numbers are numbers by any mathematical standard. They satisfy the field axioms, they are algebraically closed (every polynomial has a root), and they are indispensable in physics and engineering. The background cases were all linearly ordered real numbers; the definition took linear orderability as essential, when the concept of number has long extended beyond the one-dimensional.

background_cases: The integers under addition form a group: addition is associative, 0 is the identity, and every integer has an additive inverse. The group has infinitely many elements.

The non-zero rationals under multiplication form a group: multiplication is associative, 1 is the identity, and every non-zero rational has a multiplicative inverse. The group has infinitely many elements.
The real numbers under addition form a group with the same properties and likewise have infinitely many elements.
definition: A set with a binary operation forms a group if and only if (i) the operation is associative, (ii) there is an identity element, (iii) every element has an inverse, and (iv) the group has infinitely many elements.
conditions: operation is associative
identity element exists
every element has inverse
group has infinitely many elements
counterexample: The set {0, 1, 2} under addition modulo 3 satisfies the first three conditions: addition modulo 3 is associative, 0 is the identity, and the inverse of 1 is 2 and vice versa. But the set has only three elements. The fourth condition fails, yet this is a perfectly legitimate group — the cyclic group of order 3, which is of fundamental importance in algebra and number theory.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all infinite groups drawn from the familiar number systems. The definition abstracted infinitude as essential, but finite groups exist in profusion and are at least as important as infinite ones — the classification of finite simple groups is one of the great achievements of twentieth-century mathematics.
passage:
Consider three groups. The integers under addition, with identity 0 and inverses given by negation, form an infinite group. The non-zero rationals under multiplication, with identity 1 and inverses given by reciprocals, form an infinite group. The reals under addition likewise form an infinite group. From these cases one might define a group: a set with a binary operation that is associative, has an identity element, has inverses for every element, and has infinitely many elements.

But this definition, drawn from the infinite number-theoretic groups, proves too restrictive. Consider the set {0, 1, 2} under addition modulo 3. The operation is associative. The identity element is 0. The inverse of 0 is 0, the inverse of 1 is 2, and the inverse of 2 is 1. The first three conditions are satisfied. But the set has only three elements, so the fourth condition fails. Yet this is a perfectly good group — the cyclic group Z/3Z, which plays a central role in algebra, number theory, and the theory of symmetry. The background cases were all infinite; the definition mistook infinitude for a necessary feature. But finite groups are ubiquitous in mathematics: the symmetric groups, the dihedral groups, and the simple groups classified in the monumental theorem of the twentieth century are all finite, and they are no less genuine for having only finitely many elements.

background_cases: Two integers are equivalent modulo 5 if they leave the same remainder upon division by 5; this relation is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.
Two triangles are equivalent (congruent) if one can be mapped onto the other by a rigid motion; this relation is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.
Two sets are equivalent (equipotent) if there exists a bijection between them; this relation is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.
definition: A relation is an equivalence relation if and only if (i) it is reflexive, (ii) it is symmetric, (iii) it is transitive, and (iv) each equivalence class contains more than one element.
conditions: reflexive
symmetric
transitive
each equivalence class contains more than one element
counterexample: The identity relation on any set — the relation that holds between each element and itself alone — is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive. It is an equivalence relation. But each equivalence class is a singleton: {x} for each element x. The fourth condition fails, yet the identity relation is the most fundamental equivalence relation of all.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved equivalence relations whose classes grouped multiple distinct elements together. The definition assumed that grouping distinct elements was essential, but the identity relation demonstrates that an equivalence relation may partition a set into singletons, with each element equivalent only to itself.
passage:
Consider three equivalence relations. Congruence modulo 5 groups integers into classes of those sharing the same remainder. Geometric congruence groups triangles that can be superimposed by rigid motions. Equipotence groups sets of the same cardinality. In each case, the equivalence classes contain many distinct elements that are treated as equivalent in the relevant respect. From these cases one might define an equivalence relation: it is reflexive, symmetric, transitive, and each equivalence class contains more than one element.

But this definition, drawn from relations that group distinct objects together, excludes a limiting case of great importance. Consider the identity relation on a set: the relation that holds between each element and itself alone. It is reflexive (every element is identical to itself), symmetric (if a = b then b = a), and transitive (if a = b and b = c then a = c). It is, by the standard definition, an equivalence relation. But each equivalence class is a singleton — the class of a contains only a. The fourth condition fails. Yet the identity relation is the most fundamental equivalence relation there is; it is the finest possible partition of any set, and every other equivalence relation is a coarsening of it. The background cases all involved coarse partitions with multiple elements per class; the definition mistook this for a necessary feature, when the concept of equivalence admits the degenerate case in which each element is equivalent only to itself.

background_cases: A square has eight symmetries: four rotations (by 0, 90, 180, and 270 degrees) and four reflections. Each symmetry is a rigid motion of the plane.
An equilateral triangle has six symmetries: three rotations and three reflections. Each is a rigid motion.
A regular hexagon has twelve symmetries: six rotations and six reflections. Each is a rigid motion.
definition: The symmetry group of a geometric figure consists of all and only the transformations such that (i) each is a distance-preserving map of the plane, (ii) each maps the figure onto itself, and (iii) the transformations form a finite group under composition.
conditions: each transformation is distance-preserving
each maps figure onto itself
transformations form finite group under composition
counterexample: Consider an infinite strip with a repeating pattern — a frieze. The symmetry group of the frieze includes translations along the strip by any integer multiple of the pattern's period. These translations are distance-preserving and map the frieze onto itself, but there are infinitely many of them. The transformations do not form a finite group; the group is infinite. The third condition fails, yet the symmetries of friezes are a standard topic in the mathematical classification of symmetry.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all bounded figures with finitely many symmetries. The definition abstracted finiteness of the symmetry group as essential, but unbounded figures such as friezes and wallpaper patterns have infinite symmetry groups that are equally legitimate objects of mathematical study.
passage:
Consider three symmetric figures. A square has eight symmetries forming a finite group. An equilateral triangle has six symmetries forming a finite group. A regular hexagon has twelve symmetries forming a finite group. From these cases one might define the symmetry group of a figure: it consists of all distance-preserving maps that send the figure to itself, and the transformations form a finite group under composition.

But this definition, drawn from bounded polygonal figures, fails for unbounded patterns. Consider a frieze — an infinite strip bearing a repeating motif, such as the decorative bands found in architecture and textile design. The symmetry group of the frieze includes translations along the strip by any integer multiple of the pattern's period. Each such translation is distance-preserving and maps the frieze onto itself. But there are infinitely many such translations, and the symmetry group is therefore infinite. The third condition fails. Yet frieze symmetries are a standard and important topic in mathematics: there are exactly seven distinct frieze groups, classified by the types of symmetry they admit (translation, reflection, glide reflection, rotation). The background cases were bounded figures with finite symmetry groups; the definition treated finiteness as essential. But the mathematical study of symmetry extends naturally to infinite groups, and the symmetries of friezes and wallpaper patterns are among the most beautiful applications of group theory to geometry.

background_cases: The set {2, 4, 6, 8} is defined by listing its elements; each element is a definite, well-distinguished object.
The set of all prime numbers less than 20 is defined by a property; membership is determinate for each natural number.
The set of vertices of a given triangle is defined by enumeration; it has exactly three elements.
definition: A collection is a well-defined set if and only if (i) its elements are determinate, (ii) for any object, it is decidable whether the object belongs to the collection, and (iii) the collection is countable.
conditions: elements are determinate
membership is decidable for any object
collection is countable
counterexample: The set of all real numbers is a well-defined set: its elements are determinate (each real number is a definite mathematical object), and for any mathematical entity one can determine whether or not it is a real number. But the set of real numbers is uncountable — Cantor proved that there is no bijection between the reals and the natural numbers. The third condition fails, yet the set of real numbers is one of the most fundamental objects in all of mathematics.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all finite or at most countably infinite sets. The definition assumed that every well-defined set is countable, but Cantor's diagonal argument shows that uncountable sets exist and are indispensable to analysis, measure theory, and topology.
passage:
Consider three sets. The set {2, 4, 6, 8} is defined by listing its four elements. The set of primes less than 20 is defined by a property and has eight members. The set of vertices of a triangle has exactly three elements. Each is countable — indeed, each is finite. From these cases one might define a well-defined set: its elements are determinate, membership is decidable, and the collection is countable.

But this definition, drawn from small, finite collections, proves too restrictive. Consider the set of all real numbers. Each real number is a determinate mathematical object. For any entity one can determine whether it is a real number. But the set of real numbers is not countable. Cantor demonstrated in 1874, and more famously by his diagonal argument of 1891, that the real numbers cannot be placed in one-to-one correspondence with the natural numbers. The set of reals is uncountably infinite. The third condition fails. Yet the set of real numbers is perhaps the most important single set in mathematics — it underlies analysis, topology, measure theory, and the mathematical description of the physical world. The background cases were all finite; the definition mistook countability for a necessary condition. But the concept of a set is not limited by countability, and some of the most significant sets in mathematics are irremediably uncountable.

background_cases: The function f(x) = x^2 sends each real number to its square; for each input there is exactly one output, and nearby inputs produce nearby outputs.
The function g(x) = sin(x) sends each real number to its sine; the function is smooth and continuous everywhere.
The function h(x) = e^x sends each real number to its exponential; the function is smooth, continuous, and everywhere differentiable.
definition: A mapping from the reals to the reals is a genuine function if and only if (i) it assigns exactly one output to each input, (ii) it is continuous, and (iii) it is differentiable at every point.
conditions: assigns exactly one output to each input
continuous
differentiable at every point
counterexample: The absolute value function f(x) = |x| assigns each real number a unique output, and it is continuous everywhere. The series 1 + 1/3 + 1/9 + 1/27 + ..., in which each term is one-third of its predecessor, converges to 3/2.
