From these cases one might extract a definition: an infinite series converges if and only if there exists a constant ratio r with 0 < r < 1 such that each term equals r times its predecessor, every term is positive, and the first term is finite.

But one must consider the series 1 + 1/4 + 1/9 + 1/16 + ..., whose general term is 1/n^2. This series converges; its sum is equal to pi squared divided by six, a result first established by Euler and subsequently confirmed by numerous independent proofs. Every term is positive and the first term is finite. Yet the ratio of successive terms is n^2 divided by (n+1)^2, which varies with n: the ratio of the second term to the first is 1/4, the ratio of the third to the second is 4/9, the ratio of the fourth to the third is 9/16, and so on. There is no constant ratio r governing the relationship between successive terms. The first condition of the definition fails. The definition was abstracted from geometric series, in which a single constant ratio governs the decay of the terms and provides a simple formula for the sum. But convergence is not confined to the geometric pattern. A series may converge because its terms decrease with sufficient rapidity, even though the rate of decrease varies from term to term and obeys no fixed ratio. The definition mistook the particular mechanism of convergence in geometric series — the constant ratio — for a necessary condition of convergence as such, overlooking the vast and varied domain of convergent series whose terms diminish by no single rule.

background_cases: The set {1, 2, 3} and the set {a, b, c} have the same cardinality, as shown by the bijection 1 -> a, 2 -> b, 3 -> c.
The set of days of the week and the set of colours of the rainbow each contain seven elements, and a bijection between them is easily constructed.
The set of vertices of a regular hexagon and the set {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6} have the same cardinality, as verified by enumerating the vertices.
definition: Two sets A and B have the same cardinality if and only if (i) A and B are both finite, (ii) one can list the elements of A and the elements of B and verify that the lists have the same length, and (iii) there exists a bijection from A to B.
conditions: A and B are both finite
one can list the elements of A and B and verify equal length
there exists a bijection from A to B
counterexample: Consider the set of natural numbers N = {0, 1, 2, 3, ...} and the set of integers Z = {..., -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, ...}. Neither set is finite, and neither can be listed in its entirety. Yet there exists a bijection from N to Z — for instance, the function that sends each even natural number 2k to k and each odd natural number 2k + 1 to -(k + 1). This bijection establishes that N and Z have the same cardinality. The first and second conditions fail, yet the two sets are equinumerous.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all pairs of small finite sets whose elements could be explicitly listed and matched. The definition abstracted finiteness and listability as conditions of equinumerosity because they were features of every case examined. But the cases were drawn exclusively from the domain of finite sets, where counting and listing are available. The case of the natural numbers and the integers reveals that the concept of equinumerosity — sameness of cardinality — extends beyond the finite. Two infinite sets may have the same cardinality if there exists a bijection between them, even though neither set can be listed or counted in the ordinary sense. The definition mistook the features of finite equinumerosity for the features of equinumerosity as such, failing to accommodate the extension of the concept to the infinite that is one of the foundational achievements of set theory.
passage:
Consider three pairs of sets that have the same cardinality. The set {1, 2, 3} and the set {a, b, c}: one lists the elements of each, observes that both lists contain three entries, and constructs the bijection that sends 1 to a, 2 to b, and 3 to c. The set of days of the week and the set of colours of the rainbow: each contains seven elements, and a bijection is readily exhibited. The set of vertices of a regular hexagon and the set {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}: one enumerates the vertices, finds six, and pairs them with the six numbers. From these cases one might extract a definition: two sets have the same cardinality if and only if both are finite, one can list their elements and verify that the lists have the same length, and there exists a bijection between them.

But one must consider the natural numbers and the integers. The set of natural numbers — 0, 1, 2, 3, and so on without end — is not finite, and its elements cannot be listed in their entirety, for the listing would never be completed. The set of integers — extending without limit in both the positive and the negative direction — is likewise infinite and unlistable. The first two conditions of the definition fail for both sets. Yet the natural numbers and the integers have the same cardinality. One may construct a bijection as follows: send each even natural number 2k to the integer k, and send each odd natural number 2k + 1 to the integer -(k + 1). Under this mapping, 0 goes to 0, 1 goes to -1, 2 goes to 1, 3 goes to -2, 4 goes to 2, and so on, establishing a perfect one-to-one correspondence between the two sets. The definition, drawn from cases of small finite sets whose elements can be laid out and counted, imposed finiteness and listability as necessary conditions, when in truth the concept of equinumerosity extends naturally and rigorously to the infinite. The bijective criterion — the third condition — is the one that does the essential work, and it applies to finite and infinite sets alike.

background_cases: The problem of determining whether a given natural number is even is decidable: one divides by 2 and checks whether the remainder is zero.
The problem of determining whether a given natural number is prime is decidable: one tests divisibility by all natural numbers from 2 up to the square root of the given number.

The problem of determining whether a given string of parentheses is balanced is decidable: one traverses the string, maintaining a counter that is incremented for each opening parenthesis and decremented for each closing one, accepting if and only if the counter never becomes negative and ends at zero.
definition: A decision problem P is decidable if and only if (i) there exists an algorithm that solves P, (ii) the algorithm can be explained in ordinary language to a competent human being, and (iii) the algorithm proceeds by examining the input one element at a time, from left to right, making a decision at each step.
conditions: there exists an algorithm that solves P
the algorithm can be explained in ordinary language
the algorithm proceeds by examining the input one element at a time from left to right
counterexample: Consider the problem of determining whether a given finite graph is two-colourable — that is, whether its vertices can be coloured with two colours such that no two adjacent vertices share the same colour. This problem is decidable: one can systematically enumerate all possible two-colourings and check each. But no algorithm that proceeds by examining the vertices one at a time, from left to right, making a decision at each step, suffices to solve this problem in general, for the colourability of a graph depends upon global properties — the structure of cycles, the relationships among distant vertices — that cannot be determined by a single left-to-right pass. The third condition fails, yet the problem is decidable.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all problems solvable by simple, single-pass, left-to-right algorithms — checking evenness, checking primality by sequential trial division, checking balanced parentheses by a counter. The definition abstracted the left-to-right, one-element-at-a-time structure as essential to decidability because every algorithm in the background cases exhibited this pattern. But the cases were drawn from a narrow class of problems admitting particularly simple algorithmic solutions. The graph two-colourability problem reveals that decidable problems may require algorithms that examine the input in complex, non-sequential patterns, making multiple passes, backtracking, or consulting global properties that no single left-to-right scan can detect. The definition mistook the sequential, one-pass structure of simple algorithms for a necessary feature of algorithmic solvability.
passage:
Consider three decidable problems. Determining whether a natural number is even: one divides by 2 and checks the remainder, a procedure that examines the input directly and yields a verdict in a single step. Determining whether a natural number is prime: one tests divisibility by each integer from 2 up to the square root of the given number, proceeding in order, and accepts if no divisor is found. Determining whether a string of parentheses is balanced: one traverses the string from left to right, maintaining a counter, and accepts if the counter never becomes negative and ends at zero. In each case, the algorithm examines the input one element at a time, proceeds in a fixed left-to-right direction, and makes a determination at each step. From these cases one might extract a definition: a decision problem is decidable if and only if there exists an algorithm that solves it, the algorithm can be explained in ordinary language, and the algorithm proceeds by examining the input one element at a time, from left to right, making a decision at each step.

But one must consider the problem of determining whether a finite graph is two-colourable. A graph is two-colourable if its vertices can be assigned one of two colours — say, black and white — in such a manner that no two adjacent vertices receive the same colour. This problem is decidable; one may solve it by checking whether the graph contains an odd cycle, which can be accomplished by a breadth-first search from each connected component. The algorithm is perfectly effective and always terminates. But it does not proceed by examining the input one element at a time from left to right, making a decision at each step. A graph has no natural left-to-right ordering of its elements; the vertices are connected by edges in a structure that may be highly irregular, and the colourability of the whole depends upon global properties — the lengths of cycles, the relationships among vertices that may be far apart in any ordering one might impose. The algorithm must explore the graph's structure, following edges, backtracking, and maintaining information about the global pattern of connections. The definition, drawn from problems admitting simple sequential algorithms, mistook the left-to-right, one-pass structure of those algorithms for a necessary feature of decidability, when in truth decidability requires only that an effective procedure exist, however complex its pattern of access to the input.

background_cases: The number 15 is not prime; it is the product of 3 and 5, two smaller primes. Trial division by all integers from 2 up to 15 quickly reveals the factor 3.
The number 21 is not prime; it is the product of 3 and 7. Trial division by all integers from 2 up to 21 reveals the factor 3 at the third step.
The number 35 is not prime; it is the product of 5 and 7. Trial division by all integers from 2 up to 35 reveals the factor 5 at the fourth step.
definition: A natural number n greater than 1 is prime if and only if (i) n is not divisible by any integer k with 2 <= k <= n - 1, (ii) the smallest factor of n greater than 1 is n itself, and (iii) n cannot be expressed as the product of two natural numbers both lying strictly between 1 and n.
conditions: n is not divisible by any k with 2 <= k <= n-1
the smallest factor of n greater than 1 is n itself
n cannot be expressed as a product of two numbers both strictly between 1 and n
counterexample: Consider the numbers 2 and 3. Both are prime by universal agreement. For 2: there is no integer k with 2 <= k <= 1, so the first condition holds vacuously. The smallest factor of 2 greater than 1 is 2 itself, so the second condition holds. But 2 cannot be expressed as the product of two numbers both strictly between 1 and 2, because there are no natural numbers strictly between 1 and 2. The third condition holds vacuously. Similarly for 3. Now consider 4: it is 2 times 2, but 2 lies strictly between 1 and 4, so 4 fails the third condition. So far the definition appears to work. But one notices that the three conditions, despite their different formulations, are logically equivalent for all natural numbers greater than 1. The definition has not offered three independent conditions but has restated a single condition three times in different language, giving a false impression of depth.
abductive_insight: The background cases — 15, 21, 35 — were all composite numbers whose compositeness was demonstrated by finding a factor through trial division. The definition abstracted three conditions from this process: non-divisibility, minimality of factor, and non-expressibility as a product. But these three conditions, despite their superficially different formulations, are logically equivalent for every natural number greater than 1. Each is simply a restatement of the others. The definition, drawn from cases in which the single property of indivisibility was demonstrated by a single method, has dressed up one condition in three different verbal garments, creating the illusion of a tripartite analysis where in truth there is only a single criterion. The apparent structure of the definition is a product of linguistic variation, not of conceptual analysis.
passage:
Consider three composite numbers whose compositeness is readily established. The number 15 is not prime; trial division reveals that it is divisible by 3, and indeed 15 equals 3 times 5. The number 21 is not prime; it is divisible by 3, and 21 equals 3 times 7. The number 35 is not prime; it is divisible by 5, and 35 equals 5 times 7. In each case, the compositeness of the number is demonstrated by finding a factor — a number strictly between 1 and the number itself that divides it evenly. From these cases one might extract a definition: a natural number greater than 1 is prime if and only if it is not divisible by any integer k with 2 at most equal to k and k at most equal to n minus 1, the smallest factor of the number greater than 1 is the number itself, and the number cannot be expressed as the product of two natural numbers both lying strictly between 1 and the number.

The definition presents three conditions that appear to constitute an independent tripartite analysis. Yet upon closer inspection, the three conditions are logically equivalent for every natural number greater than 1. To say that n has no divisor between 2 and n minus 1 is to say that the smallest factor of n greater than 1 is n itself, which is to say that n cannot be written as a product of two numbers both strictly between 1 and n. Each condition is a reformulation of the others; they differ in their verbal expression but not in their logical content. The definition has not identified three independent necessary conditions whose conjunction is sufficient for primality; it has identified one condition and stated it three times in different words. The background cases — 15, 21, 35 — were all instances in which the single property of divisibility by a smaller number was demonstrated by a single procedure, namely trial division. The definition, drawn from these cases, has presented the result of that single procedure in three different formulations, creating the impression of analytic depth where in truth there is only verbal variation. The analysis mistakes linguistic diversity for conceptual multiplicity.

background_cases: The polynomial function f(x) = x^2 is continuous on the entire real line; at every point a, the function is defined, the limit exists, and the limit equals the value of the function. Moreover, f preserves the convergence of sequences: if x_n converges to a, then f(x_n) converges to f(a).
The exponential function f(x) = e^x is continuous on the entire real line; it is defined everywhere, preserves limits, and maps convergent sequences to convergent sequences.

The sine function f(x) = sin(x) is continuous on the entire real line; at every point, the limit of the function equals the value of the function, and the function maps convergent sequences to convergent sequences.
definition: A function f from the real numbers to the real numbers is continuous at a point a if and only if (i) f is defined at a, (ii) for every sequence (x_n) converging to a, the sequence (f(x_n)) converges to f(a), and (iii) f maps bounded sets to bounded sets in every neighbourhood of a.
conditions: f is defined at a
for every sequence converging to a, the image sequence converges to f(a)
f maps bounded sets to bounded sets in every neighbourhood of a
counterexample: Consider the function f(x) = x^2 on the entire real line. This function is continuous everywhere by any standard criterion. Now consider a bounded set, say the closed interval [0, n], and the function g(x) = x^2. The image of [0, n] under g is [0, n^2], which is bounded. So for polynomial functions the third condition tends to be satisfied on bounded sets. But consider instead the function h(x) = x, the identity function, which is continuous everywhere. It trivially satisfies the third condition. The difficulty arises when one considers the converse direction: a function that satisfies the first two conditions but fails the third. Consider f(x) = 1/x for x not equal to 0, with f(0) = 0. This function is continuous at every point except 0 in the standard sense. At the point 0 it fails the second condition (and so is not continuous there), but this means the third condition adds nothing to the analysis at points of continuity. For all functions continuous at a point, the third condition is automatically satisfied. The third condition is redundant, adding no content beyond what the first two conditions already provide.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the polynomial, the exponential, the sine function — were all continuous functions that happened to map bounded sets to bounded sets in neighbourhoods of every point. The definition abstracted this boundedness-preserving property as a separate necessary condition for continuity. But the property is not independent of the sequential characterisation of continuity; it is a consequence of it. If a function satisfies the sequential criterion at a point — if every convergent sequence mapping to the point has its image converging to the function value — then the function necessarily maps bounded sets to bounded sets in every neighbourhood of the point. The third condition, extracted from the background cases as though it were an independent feature, is in truth a logical consequence of the second condition. The definition has mistaken a theorem about continuous functions for an independent axiom of continuity.
passage:
Consider three functions that are paradigmatically continuous. The polynomial function defined by the square of the argument is continuous at every point of the real line. It is defined everywhere; for every sequence converging to a given point, the images converge to the square of that point; and it maps bounded sets to bounded sets. The exponential function is continuous at every point. It is defined everywhere; it preserves the convergence of sequences; and it maps bounded sets to bounded sets. The sine function is continuous at every point. It is defined everywhere; it preserves sequential convergence; and it is itself bounded, so it trivially maps bounded sets to bounded sets. From these cases one might extract a definition: a function is continuous at a point if and only if it is defined there, it preserves the convergence of sequences to that point, and it maps bounded sets to bounded sets in every neighbourhood of the point.

The first two conditions constitute the well-known sequential characterisation of continuity, which is equivalent to the standard epsilon-delta definition and is universally accepted as correct. The third condition — that the function maps bounded sets to bounded sets in neighbourhoods of the point — is satisfied by all three background cases and might therefore seem an independent necessary condition. Yet it is not independent. It is a consequence of the sequential characterisation. If a function preserves the convergence of every sequence converging to a point, then it is bounded on every sufficiently small neighbourhood of that point, and hence it maps bounded subsets of such neighbourhoods to bounded sets. The third condition adds no content that is not already entailed by the second. The background cases were all well-behaved functions that exhibited many pleasant properties beyond mere continuity, and the definition has extracted one such property — boundedness preservation — and elevated it to the status of an independent condition, when in truth it follows from the sequential criterion as a logical consequence. The analysis has mistaken a theorem about continuous functions for an independent axiom, inflating the definition with a redundant condition that creates an appearance of additional rigour without adding any genuine content.

background_cases: The set {1, 2, 3} has three elements and the set {a, b, c} has three elements; by pairing 1 with a, 2 with b, and 3 with c, one establishes a bijection, and the two sets have the same cardinality.
The set of vowels {a, e, i, o, u} has five elements and the set {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} has five elements; by pairing them in the obvious way one establishes a bijection, confirming equal cardinality.
The set {red, blue} has two elements and the set {0, 1} has two elements; a bijection is easily constructed, and the two sets have the same cardinality.
definition: A set A has strictly greater cardinality than a set B if and only if (i) there exists an injection from B into A — that is, every element of B can be paired with a distinct element of A, (ii) A has elements left over after the pairing — that is, the injection is not a surjection, and (iii) A has more elements than B in the sense that no bijection from B to A can be constructed.
conditions: there exists an injection from B into A
the injection is not a surjection — A has elements left over
no bijection from B to A can be constructed
counterexample: Consider the set of natural numbers N = {0, 1, 2, 3, ...} and the set of even natural numbers E = {0, 2, 4, 6, ...}. There is an injection from E into N — the inclusion map that sends each even number to itself — and this injection is not a surjection, for the odd numbers are left over. The first two conditions are satisfied. Yet the third condition fails: there is a bijection from E to N, namely the function that sends each even number 2k to k. The sets have the same cardinality, despite the fact that an injection from E into N leaves elements of N unpaired. The definition has assumed that the existence of a non-surjective injection establishes a strict difference in cardinality, but this assumption fails for infinite sets.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all finite sets — {1,2,3}, {a,e,i,o,u}, {red,blue} — for which the existence of a non-surjective injection from B into A is both necessary and sufficient for A to have strictly greater cardinality than B. If A has more elements than B in the finite case, then no bijection between them can exist, and every injection from B into A must leave elements of A unpaired. The definition abstracted these features as essential to the comparison of cardinalities. But the cases were drawn exclusively from the finite domain, and the definition failed to anticipate the behaviour of infinite sets. For infinite sets, the existence of a non-surjective injection from B into A does not imply that no bijection exists. The set of even numbers injects into the natural numbers with odd numbers left over, yet the two sets are equinumerous. The definition confused a feature of finite cardinality comparison with a feature of cardinality comparison in general.
passage:
Consider three pairs of finite sets whose cardinalities are compared by constructing bijections. The set containing the numbers 1, 2, and 3 and the set containing the letters a, b, and c have the same cardinality; a bijection is constructed by pairing 1 with a, 2 with b, and 3 with c, and no elements are left over on either side. The set of vowels and the set containing the first five positive integers have the same cardinality, established by an obvious pairing. The set containing two colours and the set containing two digits have the same cardinality. From these finite cases one might extract a criterion for strict inequality of cardinality: a set A has strictly greater cardinality than a set B if and only if there exists an injection from B into A, this injection is not a surjection — so that elements of A are left over — and no bijection from B to A can be constructed.

The first two conditions together seem, on the evidence of the finite cases, to entail the third. If one can pair every element of B with a distinct element of A and still have elements of A left over, then surely A has more elements than B, and no bijection between them can exist. This reasoning is impeccable for finite sets. But one must consider the set of natural numbers and the set of even natural numbers. There is an injection from the even numbers into the natural numbers — the inclusion map that sends each even number to itself — and this injection is not a surjection, for the odd numbers are left over. The first two conditions are satisfied. Yet the third condition fails. There is a bijection from the even numbers to the natural numbers: the function that sends each even number 2k to k pairs every even number with a unique natural number and leaves no natural number unpaired. The two sets have the same cardinality, despite the fact that one is a proper subset of the other. The background cases were all finite, and in the finite domain, the existence of a non-surjective injection is sufficient to establish a strict difference in cardinality. The definition abstracted this sufficiency as a general principle. But infinite sets do not obey the principle that a proper subset must have fewer elements than the whole; an infinite set can be placed in bijection with a proper subset of itself. The definition mistook a feature of finite cardinality for a universal law.

background_cases: The number 7 is prime; it is divisible only by 1 and by itself, and when one attempts to divide it by any integer between 1 and 7, a remainder is always left.
The number 13 is prime; no integer other than 1 and 13 divides it evenly, and it cannot be expressed as a product of two smaller natural numbers each greater than 1.
The number 23 is prime; it admits no divisors other than 1 and itself, and trial division by every integer up to its square root confirms this.
The number 31 is prime; it is not the product of any two natural numbers both greater than 1, and it resists factorisation.
definition: A natural number n greater than 1 is prime if and only if (i) n is odd, (ii) n is not divisible by any natural number other than 1 and itself, and (iii) n cannot be expressed as a product of two natural numbers each greater than 1.
conditions: n is odd
n is not divisible by any natural number other than 1 and itself
n cannot be expressed as a product of two natural numbers each greater than 1
counterexample: Consider the number 2. It is greater than 1. It is not divisible by any natural number other than 1 and itself — there is no integer between 1 and 2 that divides it. It cannot be expressed as a product of two natural numbers each greater than 1. By conditions (ii) and (iii) it qualifies as prime. And indeed, 2 is universally recognised as the smallest prime number. Yet 2 is even, not odd. Condition (i) fails. The definition excludes 2 from the primes, contradicting the universal mathematical consensus that 2 is prime.
abductive_insight: The background cases — 7, 13, 23, 31 — were all odd primes. The definition abstracted oddness as an essential feature of primality because every example in the background happened to be odd. But the background cases were drawn, whether by accident or by unconscious selection, from the vast class of odd primes, and the one even prime — 2 — was not represented. The definition mistook a feature that is shared by all primes except one for a feature that is shared by all primes without exception. The number 2 reveals that oddness is an accidental property of almost all primes, not an essential property of primality itself.
passage:
Consider four numbers that are universally recognised as prime. The number 7 is divisible only by 1 and by itself; it cannot be factored into smaller natural numbers greater than 1. The number 13 admits no divisors other than 1 and 13. The number 23 resists division by every integer between 1 and 23; trial division up to its square root confirms that it has no non-trivial factors. The number 31 is likewise indivisible — no product of two natural numbers greater than 1 yields 31. From these cases one might observe that each number is odd, each is divisible only by 1 and by itself, and each cannot be expressed as a product of two smaller integers. One might therefore propose that a natural number greater than 1 is prime if and only if it is odd, is divisible only by 1 and by itself, and cannot be so factored.

But one must consider the number 2. It is greater than 1. It is divisible only by 1 and by itself — there is no natural number between 1 and 2 that could serve as a divisor. It cannot be expressed as a product of two natural numbers each greater than 1, for the only factorisation of 2 is 1 times 2. By conditions (ii) and (iii) it qualifies as prime. And it is, indeed, universally acknowledged by mathematicians to be the smallest prime number. It is the first entry in every table of primes, the foundation upon which the fundamental theorem of arithmetic rests. Yet 2 is even, not odd. The first condition of the definition excludes it. The background cases were 7, 13, 23, and 31 — all odd numbers. The definition abstracted oddness as an essential property of primes because every example happened to exhibit it. But the examples were drawn, whether by design or by inadvertence, from the overwhelming majority of primes that are odd, and the single even prime — the number 2 — was absent from the sample. The definition mistook a property shared by all primes save one for a property essential to primality itself. The number 2 reveals that oddness is an almost universal but not an invariable feature of prime numbers, and that a definition which demands it will exclude the very number upon which the theory of primes begins.

background_cases: The function f(x) = x^2 is continuous at every point; its graph is an unbroken parabola, and small changes in x produce small changes in f(x).
The function f(x) = sin(x) is continuous at every point; its graph is a smooth, unbroken wave, and the function varies gradually as x varies.
The function f(x) = 1/(1 + x^2) is continuous at every point; its graph is a smooth bell-shaped curve that never breaks or jumps.
definition: A function f from the real numbers to the real numbers is continuous at a point c if and only if (i) f is defined at c, (ii) the limit of f(x) as x approaches c exists and equals f(c), and (iii) f is differentiable at c — that is, the derivative f'(c) exists.
conditions: f is defined at c
the limit of f(x) as x approaches c equals f(c)
f is differentiable at c
counterexample: Consider the function f(x) = |x|, the absolute value function. At c = 0, f is defined: f(0) = 0. The limit of f(x) as x approaches 0 exists and equals 0 = f(0). Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. The function is continuous at 0 — its graph passes through the origin without a break or a jump. Yet f is not differentiable at 0: the graph has a sharp corner at the origin, and the left-hand and right-hand derivatives do not agree. The left-hand derivative is -1 and the right-hand derivative is +1. Condition (iii) fails. The function is continuous at 0 but not differentiable there, and the definition wrongly excludes it from the class of functions continuous at that point.
abductive_insight: The background cases — x^2, sin(x), 1/(1 + x^2) — were all smooth, differentiable functions. The definition abstracted differentiability as essential to continuity because every example in the background happened to be differentiable. But the background cases were drawn from the class of smooth functions, and the definition mistook a property that those particular functions possessed — differentiability — for a necessary condition of continuity. The absolute value function reveals that continuity and differentiability are distinct properties: every differentiable function is continuous, but a function may be continuous at a point without being differentiable there. The definition confused a sufficient condition for continuity with a necessary one.
passage:
Consider three functions that are paradigmatically continuous. The function f(x) = x^2 is continuous at every real number; its graph is a smooth, unbroken parabola, and small changes in x produce correspondingly small changes in f(x). The function f(x) = sin(x) is continuous everywhere; its graph is a smooth wave that never breaks or jumps. The function f(x) = 1/(1 + x^2) is continuous everywhere; its graph is a smooth bell-shaped curve. From these cases one might extract a definition: a function is continuous at a point if and only if it is defined there, the limit equals the function's value, and the function is differentiable at that point. The three background functions are not merely continuous but differentiable — indeed, infinitely differentiable — and one might suppose that differentiability is an essential component of continuity, a condition without which the smooth, unbroken character of a continuous function cannot be secured.

But one must consider the function f(x) = |x|, the absolute value function. At the point c = 0, the function is defined: f(0) = 0. The limit of f(x) as x approaches 0 exists and equals 0, which is the value of f at 0. The graph of the function passes through the origin without a break, a jump, or a gap. Small changes in x near 0 produce small changes in |x|. The function is, by any standard account, continuous at 0. Yet it is not differentiable there. The graph has a sharp corner at the origin — a vertex, a point at which the slope changes abruptly from -1 to +1. The left-hand derivative is -1 and the right-hand derivative is +1, and since these do not agree, no derivative exists at 0. Condition (iii) fails. The definition would exclude f(x) = |x| from the class of functions continuous at 0, which contradicts the established mathematical fact that |x| is continuous everywhere. The background cases were all smooth functions — functions whose graphs have no corners, no cusps, no points of non-differentiability — and the definition mistook the smoothness of these particular examples for a necessary condition of continuity in general. The absolute value function reveals that continuity requires only that the function be defined, that the limit exist, and that the two agree — and that differentiability, whilst sufficient for continuity, is not necessary for it.

background_cases: The sequence 1, 1/2, 1/3, 1/4, ... converges to 0; its terms are all positive and decrease steadily towards the limit.
The sequence 1, 1/4, 1/9, 1/16, ... (that is, 1/n^2) converges to 0; its terms are positive and strictly decreasing.
The sequence 0.9, 0.99, 0.999, 0.9999, ... converges to 1; its terms are positive, strictly increasing, and approach the limit from below.
definition: A sequence (a_n) of real numbers converges to a limit L if and only if (i) the terms of the sequence eventually become arbitrarily close to L, (ii) L is a real number, and (iii) the sequence approaches L from one side — that is, the terms are eventually all greater than L or eventually all less than L.
conditions: the terms eventually become arbitrarily close to L
L is a real number
the terms eventually approach L from one side
counterexample: Consider the sequence defined by a_n = (-1)^n / n^2, which yields the terms -1, 1/4, -1/9, 1/16, -1/25, and so forth. This sequence converges to 0: for any positive epsilon, there exists an N beyond which every term lies within epsilon of 0. The limit 0 is a real number. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. Yet the sequence does not approach 0 from one side. The terms alternate in sign — negative, positive, negative, positive — and no matter how far along the sequence one proceeds, there are always both positive and negative terms ahead. The sequence oscillates about its limit rather than approaching it from above or from below. Condition (iii) fails. Yet the sequence converges, and its convergence is beyond dispute.
abductive_insight: The background cases — 1/n, 1/n^2, the sequence of nines — were all monotone sequences that approached their limits from a single direction. The definition abstracted one-sided approach as essential to convergence. But the background cases were drawn from the class of monotone sequences, and the definition mistook a feature of that class — the unidirectional approach to the limit — for a necessary condition of convergence as such. The alternating sequence (-1)^n / n^2 reveals that a sequence may converge by oscillating about its limit, approaching from both sides simultaneously, without ever settling into a one-sided approach. The definition confused the behaviour of one species of convergent sequences with the behaviour of convergent sequences in general.
passage:
Consider three sequences that converge. The sequence 1, 1/2, 1/3, 1/4, ... converges to 0; its terms are positive and decrease steadily towards the limit, approaching it from above. The sequence 1, 1/4, 1/9, 1/16, ... converges to 0; its terms are likewise positive and strictly decreasing, and they approach the limit from above. The sequence 0.9, 0.99, 0.999, 0.9999, ... converges to 1; its terms are positive and strictly increasing, and they approach the limit from below. In each case, the sequence approaches its limit from one side — either from above or from below — and one might suppose that this one-sided approach is an essential feature of convergence. From these cases one might extract a definition: a sequence converges to a limit if the terms eventually become arbitrarily close to the limit, the limit is a real number, and the sequence approaches the limit from one side.

But one must consider the sequence defined by a_n = (-1)^n / n^2, whose terms are -1, 1/4, -1/9, 1/16, -1/25, 1/36, and so forth. This sequence converges to 0. For any positive epsilon, however small, there exists a natural number N such that for all n beyond N, the absolute value of a_n is less than epsilon — for |a_n| = 1/n^2, which tends to 0. The limit is the real number 0. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. Yet the sequence does not approach 0 from one side. The odd-indexed terms are negative — they lie below 0 — and the even-indexed terms are positive — they lie above 0. No matter how far along the sequence one goes, the terms continue to alternate between values below the limit and values above it. The sequence oscillates about its limit, approaching it simultaneously from both sides, and never settles into a one-sided approach. Condition (iii) fails. Yet the convergence of the sequence is beyond dispute: the terms cluster ever more tightly about 0, and the oscillations diminish to any desired degree. The background cases were all monotone sequences — sequences that marched steadily towards their limits from a single direction — and the definition abstracted this unidirectional behaviour as essential. The alternating sequence reveals that convergence requires only that the terms cluster about the limit, not that they approach it from one side, and that the definition mistook the behaviour of a particular class of convergent sequences for the nature of convergence itself.

background_cases: The set {1, 2, 3} has the same cardinality as the set {a, b, c}; one can pair 1 with a, 2 with b, and 3 with c, establishing a one-to-one correspondence in which every element of each set is matched.
The set of fingers on a human hand has the same cardinality as the set {Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday}; one can match each finger to a weekday, exhausting both sets.
The set {red, green, blue} has the same cardinality as the set {cat, dog, bird}; a bijection between the two sets can be constructed by pairing the elements in any order.
definition: Two sets A and B have the same cardinality if and only if (i) there exists a one-to-one correspondence between A and B — that is, a bijection from A to B, (ii) A and B are both finite, and (iii) the number of elements in A equals the number of elements in B.
conditions: there exists a bijection from A to B
A and B are both finite
the number of elements in A equals the number of elements in B
counterexample: Consider the set of natural numbers N = {0, 1, 2, 3, ...} and the set of even natural numbers E = {0, 2, 4, 6, ...}. Define a function f from N to E by f(n) = 2n. This function is a bijection: it is injective, for distinct natural numbers are mapped to distinct even numbers, and it is surjective, for every even number 2k is the image of k. Condition (i) is satisfied. Yet N and E are not finite — they are infinite sets. Condition (ii) fails. The definition excludes infinite sets from having 'the same cardinality,' but the concept of equicardinality, as developed by Cantor and universally accepted in modern mathematics, applies to infinite sets as well as to finite ones. N and E have the same cardinality — they are equinumerous — notwithstanding the fact that E is a proper subset of N.
abductive_insight: The background cases — {1,2,3} and {a,b,c}, fingers and weekdays, colours and animals — were all pairs of finite sets. The definition abstracted finiteness and element-counting as essential to equicardinality because every example involved finite collections whose elements could be tallied. But the background cases were drawn from the domain of small, finite sets, and the definition mistook the features of that domain — finiteness, countability by tallying — for features of equicardinality as such. The natural numbers and the even numbers reveal that equicardinality extends to infinite sets, where the notion of 'the number of elements' must be replaced by the existence of a bijection, and where a set may have the same cardinality as a proper subset of itself.

Consider three pairs of sets that are acknowledged to have the same cardinality. The set {1, 2, 3} has the same cardinality as the set {a, b, c}: one pairs 1 with a, 2 with b, 3 with c, and every element of each set is matched. The five fingers of a hand have the same cardinality as the five weekdays: each finger is paired with a weekday, and both collections are exhausted. The set {red, green, blue} has the same cardinality as {cat, dog, bird}: a bijection is readily constructed. In each case the sets are finite, and the cardinality is established by counting — by verifying that the number of elements is the same. From these cases one might extract a definition: two sets have the same cardinality if and only if there is a bijection between them, both are finite, and the number of elements is the same.

But one must consider the set of natural numbers N = {0, 1, 2, 3, ...} and the set of even natural numbers E = {0, 2, 4, 6, ...}. The function f(n) = 2n maps every natural number to a distinct even number, and every even number is the image of some natural number under this mapping. The function is a bijection from N to E: condition (i) is satisfied. Yet N and E are not finite. They are infinite collections, and one cannot assign a natural number to represent the 'number of elements' in either set. Condition (ii) fails. The definition declares that N and E do not have the same cardinality, for they are not finite. But the mathematical consensus, established by Cantor's work on transfinite cardinal numbers, is that N and E do have the same cardinality — they are equinumerous, countably infinite, and the bijection f(n) = 2n establishes their equicardinality as surely as the pairing of fingers with weekdays establishes the equicardinality of two finite sets of five. The background cases were drawn from the world of small, finite collections, and the definition mistook the features of that world for universal conditions of equicardinality. The natural numbers and the even numbers reveal that equicardinality is a concept that transcends finitude, and that the existence of a bijection, not the finiteness of the sets, is its essential criterion.

background_cases: The function f(x) = x^2 is continuous on the real line: at every point, small changes in input produce small changes in output, and the function's graph can be drawn as an unbroken curve.
The function f(x) = sin(x) is continuous on the real line: it oscillates smoothly, and at every point the limit of the function equals the function's value.
The function f(x) = 1/x, defined on the positive reals, is continuous on its domain: for any point c > 0, the limit as x approaches c equals 1/c.
The exponential function f(x) = e^x is continuous: it increases smoothly, and at every point the epsilon-delta condition is satisfied.
definition: A function f from the real numbers to the real numbers is continuous if and only if (i) f is defined at every point of its domain, (ii) the limit of f(x) as x approaches any point c in the domain exists, and (iii) the limit equals f(c) for every c in the domain.
conditions: f is defined at every point of its domain
The limit of f(x) as x approaches c exists for every c in the domain
The limit equals f(c) for every c
counterexample: Consider the indicator function of the rationals, often called Dirichlet's function: f(x) = 1 if x is rational and f(x) = 0 if x is irrational. This function is defined at every real number — condition (i) is satisfied. But the limit of f(x) as x approaches any point c does not exist, because every neighbourhood of c contains both rational and irrational numbers, so f oscillates between 0 and 1 without settling. Conditions (ii) and (iii) fail everywhere, and the definition correctly identifies the function as discontinuous. Yet consider a subtler case: the function g defined by g(x) = x if x is rational and g(x) = 0 if x is irrational. At x = 0, the limit exists and equals 0 — since both branches approach 0 — and g(0) = 0. The function is continuous at 0 but discontinuous everywhere else. The definition handles each point correctly, but the overall picture — a function continuous at exactly one point — strains the intuitive concept of continuity.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all functions that were continuous at every point of their domain and, moreover, were continuous in a uniform and well-behaved manner — small changes in input everywhere producing proportionally small changes in output. The definition captures pointwise continuity but does not distinguish between functions that are continuous everywhere in a well-behaved way and functions that exhibit the pathological behaviour of being continuous at only a single point.
passage:
The functions that serve as paradigm examples of continuity — polynomials, trigonometric functions, the exponential — share a common character. At every point of their domains, small perturbations in the input produce correspondingly small perturbations in the output. Their graphs are smooth, unbroken curves. The limit of the function at any point equals the function's value there. From these cases, one extracts the standard definition: a function is continuous if and only if it is defined at every point of its domain, the limit exists at every point, and the limit equals the function value.

Yet this definition, applied pointwise, admits cases that the paradigm examples would not have led one to anticipate. Consider the function g(x) = x for rational x and g(x) = 0 for irrational x. At the origin, both the rational and irrational branches converge to 0, and g(0) = 0. The three conditions are satisfied at x = 0, and the function is continuous there. But at every other point, the function is discontinuous — the rational and irrational branches diverge, and the limit does not equal the function value. We have a function that is continuous at exactly one point and discontinuous everywhere else. The definition handles this case correctly in the pointwise sense, but the result strains the intuitive concept of continuity almost to breaking point. The background cases were functions whose continuity was global and well-behaved; the definition, by treating continuity as a purely local, pointwise property, cannot distinguish between the smooth continuity of the exponential function and the isolated, pathological continuity of a function that is continuous at a single point. What is missing is a global or uniform notion of continuity that would separate the well-behaved functions from the pathological ones.

background_cases: The function that computes the sum of two natural numbers is computable: given any pair (m, n), one can mechanically produce m + n by repeated application of the successor operation.
The function that determines whether a natural number is prime is computable: given n, one can test divisibility by every number from 2 to the square root of n and report the result.
The function that sorts a finite list of numbers into ascending order is computable: algorithms such as insertion sort or merge sort produce the sorted list in finitely many mechanical steps.
The function that computes the greatest common divisor of two natural numbers is computable: the Euclidean algorithm produces the result through a finite sequence of divisions.
definition: A function f from the natural numbers to the natural numbers is computable if and only if (i) there exists a Turing machine M that, on input n, halts with f(n) on its tape, (ii) M halts on every input, and (iii) the description of M can be given in finitely many symbols.
conditions: A Turing machine M exists that computes f(n) for each n
M halts on every input
M is described in finitely many symbols
counterexample: Consider the busy beaver function BB(n), defined as the maximum number of 1s that any n-state Turing machine can write on a blank tape before halting. For each n, BB(n) is a well-defined natural number — there are finitely many n-state machines, and among those that halt, one writes the most 1s. A Turing machine computing BB could in principle be described in finitely many symbols if it existed; condition (iii) is not the obstacle. But no Turing machine computes BB, because BB grows faster than any computable function: for any computable function g, there exists N such that BB(n) > g(n) for all n > N. Conditions (i) and (ii) fail. The function is total, well-defined, and its values are perfectly determinate, yet it is not computable. The definition correctly excludes it, but the case reveals that there exist functions that are, in every intuitive sense, definite and determinate — each value is a specific natural number — yet lie beyond the reach of any algorithm.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved functions whose values could be computed by algorithms whose correctness was transparent and whose halting was obvious. The definition captures the formal requirement of Turing computability, but the busy beaver function reveals that there exist functions that are fully determinate — each value is a specific number — yet uncomputable. The definition does not explain why some well-defined functions are computable and others are not; it merely classifies them.
passage:
The paradigmatic computable functions — addition, primality testing, sorting, the Euclidean algorithm — share a common feature: for each, there exists a mechanical procedure that, given any valid input, produces the correct output in finitely many steps. The procedure can be described in finite terms, it halts on every input, and its correctness can be verified. From these cases, one arrives at the definition: a function is computable if and only if a Turing machine computes it on every input, halts on every input, and is finitely describable.

Yet this definition, though it correctly characterises the class of computable functions, does not illuminate the boundary between the computable and the non-computable. Consider the busy beaver function BB(n), which returns the maximum number of 1s that any halting n-state Turing machine can write on an initially blank tape. For each n, BB(n) is a perfectly well-defined natural number. There is no vagueness or ambiguity about its values — each is a specific, determinate integer. Yet no Turing machine computes BB, because BB grows faster than any computable function. The values of BB are individually knowable — for small n, they can be determined by exhaustive search — but no single algorithm captures the entire function. The definition correctly excludes BB from the class of computable functions, but the case reveals something that the paradigm examples did not suggest: that a function can be entirely determinate, with each of its values being a specific natural number, and yet be uncomputable. The background cases were functions whose computability seemed to flow from the transparency of the procedure; the busy beaver function is fully determinate but resistant to any uniform procedure. The definition classifies but does not explain: it tells us that BB is uncomputable but does not articulate what it is about the function that makes it so, beyond the bare fact that no Turing machine computes it.

background_cases: The set of even natural numbers has the same cardinality as the set of all natural numbers, because the function n -> 2n establishes a bijection between them.
The set of integers has the same cardinality as the set of natural numbers, because one can enumerate the integers as 0, 1, -1, 2, -2, 3, -3, ... establishing a bijection.
The set of rational numbers has the same cardinality as the set of natural numbers, because Cantor's diagonal enumeration provides a bijection.
definition: A set A is countably infinite if and only if (i) A is infinite — it cannot be placed in bijection with any finite set {1, 2, ..., n}, (ii) there exists a bijection between A and the natural numbers, and (iii) every infinite subset of A is also countably infinite.
conditions: A is infinite
There exists a bijection between A and the natural numbers
Every infinite subset of A is countably infinite
counterexample: Consider the set of real numbers in the interval [0, 1]. This set is infinite (condition i is met). But there is no bijection between it and the natural numbers — Cantor's diagonal argument shows that the reals are uncountable — so condition (ii) fails and the set is correctly excluded. Now consider condition (iii) applied to a set that is countably infinite, such as the natural numbers themselves. Every infinite subset of the natural numbers is indeed countably infinite — this is a theorem. Condition (iii) is satisfied, but it is redundant: it is a consequence of condition (ii), not an independent requirement. Its inclusion suggests that it adds something to the definition, when in fact it is already guaranteed by the bijection with the natural numbers. More subtly, the inclusion of condition (iii) in the definition obscures the fact that the corresponding statement fails for uncountable sets — not every infinite subset of an uncountable set is uncountable — and thus the parallel between countable and uncountable infinity is not as tidy as the definition might suggest.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved sets whose elements could be systematically listed — enumerated in a sequence indexed by the natural numbers. The definition captures this enumerability through conditions (i) and (ii), but condition (iii), though true of countably infinite sets, is a theorem rather than an independent condition; its inclusion creates the misleading impression that it is a separate requirement rather than a consequence of the bijection with the natural numbers.
passage:
The paradigmatic countably infinite sets — the even numbers, the integers, the rationals — share the property that their elements can be arranged in a sequence indexed by the natural numbers. From these cases, one might extract the definition: a set is countably infinite if and only if it is infinite, there exists a bijection between it and the natural numbers, and every infinite subset of it is also countably infinite.

The first two conditions are standard and uncontroversial. The third condition — that every infinite subset is countably infinite — is a property that the natural numbers and their equinumerous companions do indeed possess. But one might observe that condition (iii) is not independent of condition (ii); it is a consequence of it. If a set A is in bijection with the natural numbers, then every infinite subset of A can be placed in bijection with the natural numbers by composing the original bijection with an enumeration of the subset. Condition (iii) is a theorem, provable from (ii), and its inclusion in the definition creates the impression that it is an additional, independent requirement when it is in fact redundant. More importantly, the inclusion of condition (iii) obscures an asymmetry between countable and uncountable infinity. Every infinite subset of a countably infinite set is countably infinite, but the analogous statement for uncountable sets is false: the set of real numbers is uncountable, but it has countably infinite subsets, such as the natural numbers themselves. The definition, by including a consequence as though it were a condition, suggests a structural symmetry that does not exist and thereby obscures rather than illuminates the distinction between the countable and the uncountable.

background_cases: In propositional logic, every tautology is provable from the standard axioms; the system is complete in the sense that semantic truth and syntactic provability coincide.
In first-order predicate logic, Goedel's completeness theorem establishes that every logically valid sentence is provable; again, truth and provability coincide.
In the theory of real-closed fields, Tarski showed that every true sentence is decidable; the theory is complete in a strong sense.
definition: A formal system S is complete if and only if (i) every sentence that is true under the intended interpretation of S is provable in S, (ii) S is consistent — no sentence and its negation are both provable, and (iii) S has a finite set of axioms from which all provable sentences can be derived.
conditions: Every true sentence is provable in S
S is consistent
S has a finite axiom set
counterexample: Consider Peano arithmetic — the standard axiomatisation of the natural numbers. It is consistent (assuming the standard view). It has a finite set of axiom schemas (though the induction schema generates infinitely many axioms, this is sometimes loosely described as finite). Yet, by Goedel's first incompleteness theorem, there exist sentences of arithmetic that are true in the standard model but not provable in Peano arithmetic. Condition (i) fails. The system is consistent and finitely axiomatised in the relevant sense, but it is incomplete. The background cases — propositional logic, first-order logic, real-closed fields — all involved systems whose expressive power was, in a certain sense, limited: they could not express the full arithmetic of the natural numbers. The definition captures completeness as a property but does not reflect the incompleteness theorems' lesson that sufficiently powerful consistent systems cannot be complete.
abductive_insight: The background cases involved formal systems whose domains were either truth-functional (propositional logic), logically valid (first-order logic), or algebraically tame (real-closed fields). None of them was powerful enough to formalise the full arithmetic of the natural numbers. The definition abstracts a property — the coincidence of truth and provability — that holds in these limited domains but fails, by Goedel's theorem, in any consistent system capable of expressing basic arithmetic. The definition captures the property but not the boundary beyond which it cannot be achieved.
passage:
The formal systems in which completeness has been established share a common character. Propositional logic is complete: every tautology is provable. First-order predicate logic is complete: every logically valid sentence is provable. The theory of real-closed fields is complete: every true sentence about the real-closed fields is decidable. From these cases, one might extract the definition: a formal system is complete if and only if every true sentence is provable, the system is consistent, and it has a finite axiom set.

Yet this definition, abstracted from systems of limited expressive power, encounters a fundamental obstruction when applied to systems capable of expressing the arithmetic of the natural numbers. Peano arithmetic is consistent and is axiomatised by a finite set of schemas. But Goedel's first incompleteness theorem demonstrates that there exist sentences of arithmetic that are true in the standard model of the natural numbers yet not provable in Peano arithmetic. Condition (i) fails, and no consistent extension of Peano arithmetic that is recursively axiomatisable can remedy the failure — for any such extension, new true-but-unprovable sentences will arise. The background cases succeeded because their subject-matter was, in a precise sense, too weak to encode the self-referential constructions that drive Goedel's theorem. Propositional logic cannot express arithmetic; the theory of real-closed fields lacks the expressive resources to define the natural numbers. The definition captures a property that is achievable in limited domains but that Goedel's theorem shows to be unattainable in any consistent system powerful enough to formalise basic arithmetic. What the definition lacks is a recognition of this boundary — an acknowledgement that completeness is not a universal aspiration but a property whose availability depends on the expressive power of the system.

background_cases: The number 6 factors as 2 times 3 in the integers, and also as 2 times 3 in any extension of the integers to a larger ring. Its factorisation is unique (up to units and ordering), and 2 and 3 are both prime and irreducible.
The number 15 factors as 3 times 5, and this factorisation is unique. Both 3 and 5 are prime and irreducible in the integers.
The number 30 factors as 2 times 3 times 5, uniquely (up to units and ordering). Each factor is both prime and irreducible.
definition: An element p of a commutative ring R is prime if and only if (i) p is not zero, (ii) p is not a unit (that is, p has no multiplicative inverse in R), and (iii) whenever p divides a product ab in R, p divides a or p divides b.
conditions: p is not zero
p is not a unit
Whenever p divides a product ab in R, p divides a or p divides b
counterexample: Consider the ring Z[sqrt(-5)] = {a + b*sqrt(-5) : a, b in Z}. In this ring, the number 6 factors as 2 times 3, but also as (1 + sqrt(-5)) times (1 - sqrt(-5)). The element 2 is irreducible in Z[sqrt(-5)] — it cannot be expressed as a product of two non-units — yet 2 divides the product (1 + sqrt(-5))(1 - sqrt(-5)) = 6 without dividing either factor. Thus 2 satisfies conditions (i) and (ii) but fails condition (iii): it is irreducible but not prime.
abductive_insight: In the background cases, which all concern the ordinary integers, every irreducible element is also prime, so the distinction between the two properties is invisible. The counterexample, drawn from a ring lacking unique factorisation, reveals that primality (as defined by the divisibility condition) and irreducibility are genuinely distinct properties that happen to coincide in the integers.
passage:
One might observe that in the familiar ring of integers, the prime elements — 2, 3, 5, 7, and so forth — exhibit a characteristic divisibility property: whenever one of these elements divides a product, it divides at least one of the factors. The factorisations of 6 as 2 times 3, of 15 as 3 times 5, and of 30 as 2 times 3 times 5 are all unique, and in each case the factors are elements that satisfy this divisibility condition. From these cases, one might extract a definition: an element of a commutative ring is prime if and only if it is non-zero, is not a unit, and has the property that whenever it divides a product, it divides at least one of the factors.

Yet one might construct a case which reveals that this definition, though correct, captures a property that is subtly different from the more familiar notion of irreducibility — the property of not being expressible as a product of two non-units. In the integers, every irreducible element is prime and every prime is irreducible, so the two notions coincide. But consider the ring Z[sqrt(-5)], whose elements are of the form a + b*sqrt(-5) with a and b integers. In this ring, 6 admits two distinct factorisations: 2 times 3 and (1 + sqrt(-5)) times (1 - sqrt(-5)). The element 2 is irreducible — it cannot be decomposed into a product of two non-units in this ring. Yet 2 divides 6 = (1 + sqrt(-5))(1 - sqrt(-5)) without dividing either 1 + sqrt(-5) or 1 - sqrt(-5). It fails condition (iii) and is therefore not prime. What the background cases, all drawn from the integers, concealed is that primality and irreducibility are genuinely distinct algebraic properties. They coincide in rings with unique factorisation — such as the integers — but diverge in rings where unique factorisation fails. The definition of primality, with its emphasis on the divisibility condition, identifies the more fundamental of the two properties: it is primality, not mere irreducibility, that guarantees unique factorisation.

background_cases: The function f(x) = x^2 is continuous at every point: for any point c, the limit of f(x) as x approaches c exists and equals f(c). Moreover, it maps every open interval to an interval (though not necessarily an open one).
The function f(x) = sin(x) is continuous everywhere. It maps every open interval to an interval, and the preimage of every open set is open.
The function f(x) = 1/(1 + x^2) is continuous at every real number. It maps connected sets to connected sets, and the preimage of every open set under f is open.
definition: A function f from the real numbers to the real numbers is continuous if and only if (i) f is defined at every point in its domain, (ii) for every point c in the domain of f, the limit of f(x) as x approaches c exists and equals f(c), and (iii) f maps every open interval in its domain to an interval.
conditions: f is defined at every point in its domain
For every point c in the domain, the limit of f(x) as x approaches c equals f(c)
f maps every open interval in its domain to an interval
counterexample: Consider the function f defined by f(x) = x for x in [0,1) and f(x) = x + 1 for x in [1,2]. This function is defined at every point of its domain [0,2], and it maps each open interval in its domain to an interval. The limit of f(x) as x approaches any point other than 1 exists and equals f at that point. However, the limit of f(x) as x approaches 1 from the left is 1, while f(1) = 2. Condition (ii) fails at x = 1, and the function is correctly classified as discontinuous. Now consider the identity function on the rationals, f(x) = x for x in Q. It satisfies conditions (i) and (ii) on its domain, and it maps every open interval intersected with Q to an interval intersected with Q. Condition (iii) is somewhat ambiguous when the domain is not the full real line, suggesting the definition is incomplete for non-standard domains.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve functions defined on the entirety of the real line, which is itself an open and connected set. Condition (iii) — the interval-mapping property — follows from conditions (i) and (ii) for functions whose domain is an interval, by the intermediate value theorem. The definition thus contains a redundancy that becomes apparent only when one recognises that the background cases share the feature of having a connected domain.
passage:
One might observe that the standard examples of continuous functions — the squaring function, the sine function, the Lorentzian function 1/(1 + x^2) — share several properties. Each is defined at every point of its domain, each satisfies the epsilon-delta limit condition at every point, and each maps open intervals to intervals. From these observations, one might extract a definition: a real-valued function is continuous if and only if it is defined at every point of its domain, satisfies the limit condition at every point, and maps every open interval in its domain to an interval.

Yet one might observe that the three conditions are not independent. The intermediate value theorem, one of the central results of elementary analysis, establishes that every continuous function on an interval maps intervals to intervals. That is to say, condition (iii) is a consequence of conditions (i) and (ii), provided the domain is an interval — which it is in all of the background cases. The background cases thus made condition (iii) appear to be an independent requirement, when in fact it is a theorem that follows from the first two conditions together with the connectedness of the domain. This observation has two consequences. First, the definition is redundant: condition (iii) may be removed without loss when the domain is an interval. Second, the relationship between conditions (ii) and (iii) is not one of logical independence but of entailment — a fact that is concealed when one considers only functions defined on the whole real line. The case becomes more subtle when the domain is not connected, for then the intermediate value theorem does not apply, and a function may satisfy (i) and (ii) without satisfying (iii). But this subtlety arises only when one moves beyond the connected domains that the background cases implicitly presuppose.

background_cases: The geometric series 1 + 1/2 + 1/4 + 1/8 + ... converges absolutely to 2. Rearranging its terms in any order yields a series that also converges to 2.
The series 1 + 1/4 + 1/9 + 1/16 + ... (the sum of reciprocals of perfect squares) converges absolutely to pi^2/6. Any rearrangement of its terms converges to the same sum.
The series 1 - 1/4 + 1/9 - 1/16 + ... converges absolutely (since the series of absolute values converges). Any rearrangement yields the same limit.
definition: An infinite series S = a_1 + a_2 + a_3 + ... converges to a value L if and only if (i) the sequence of partial sums s_n = a_1 + ... + a_n approaches L as n approaches infinity, (ii) the series converges absolutely — that is, the series |a_1| + |a_2| + |a_3| + ... also converges, and (iii) any rearrangement of the terms of S also converges to L.
conditions: The sequence of partial sums approaches L
The series converges absolutely
Any rearrangement of the terms converges to L
counterexample: Consider the alternating harmonic series 1 - 1/2 + 1/3 - 1/4 + .... Its partial sums converge to ln(2). Yet the series of absolute values is the harmonic series 1 + 1/2 + 1/3 + ..., which diverges. Condition (ii) is not satisfied. Moreover, by Riemann's rearrangement theorem, the terms of this series can be rearranged to converge to any real number whatsoever, so condition (iii) is also violated. The series converges, but only conditionally — it satisfies condition (i) but not conditions (ii) or (iii).
abductive_insight: The background cases are all absolutely convergent series, and for such series the three conditions are equivalent: absolute convergence implies rearrangement invariance by the Riemann series theorem. The counterexample reveals that convergence of partial sums — condition (i) — is the fundamental notion, while absolute convergence and rearrangement invariance are stronger properties that happen to accompany all the background cases but are not inherent in the concept of convergence itself.

One might observe that in the most familiar examples of convergent series — the geometric series, the Basel series, and similar well-behaved series — three properties invariably hold together. From these cases, one might be tempted to incorporate all three properties into the definition of convergence, stipulating that a series converges to L if and only if its partial sums approach L, it converges absolutely, and any rearrangement also converges to L.

Yet one might construct a case which reveals that this definition is too restrictive. The alternating harmonic series 1 - 1/2 + 1/3 - 1/4 + ... has partial sums that converge to ln(2). It is a convergent series in the standard sense. Yet the series of absolute values is the harmonic series, which diverges, so condition (ii) fails. And by Riemann's rearrangement theorem, the terms of this conditionally convergent series can be rearranged to produce a series converging to any prescribed real number — or indeed to diverge — so condition (iii) also fails. The series satisfies only condition (i). What the background cases concealed, by virtue of all being absolutely convergent, is that absolute convergence and rearrangement invariance are not part of the concept of convergence but rather additional properties that some convergent series possess and others do not. The fundamental notion is the convergence of partial sums; absolute convergence is a stronger condition that guarantees rearrangement invariance, but it is not necessary for convergence. The definition, by conflating convergence with absolute convergence, excludes an entire class of genuinely convergent series — the conditionally convergent series — from the category of convergent series altogether.

background_cases: The set {1, 2, 3} and the set {a, b, c} have the same cardinality, since the function sending 1 to a, 2 to b, and 3 to c is a bijection. This accords with the intuition that both sets have three elements.

The set of natural numbers {0, 1, 2, ...} and the set of integers {..., -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, ...} have the same cardinality, since the function that interleaves non-negative and negative integers establishes a bijection. Both are countably infinite.

The set of even natural numbers {0, 2, 4, ...} and the set of all natural numbers {0, 1, 2, ...} have the same cardinality, since n maps to 2n is a bijection. This holds despite the even numbers being a proper subset.
definition: A set A is strictly larger than a set B if and only if (i) there exists an injection from B to A, (ii) there exists no bijection from B to A, and (iii) A is not a subset of B.
conditions: There exists an injection from B to A
There exists no bijection from B to A
A is not a subset of B
counterexample: Consider the set of real numbers R and the set of natural numbers N. There is plainly an injection from N to R (the inclusion map), and Cantor's diagonal argument shows that no bijection from N to R exists. Yet R is not a subset of N — condition (iii) is satisfied vacuously in the wrong direction. In fact, the definition requires that A not be a subset of B, which is irrelevant to the comparison of cardinalities. Now consider the power set P(N) of the natural numbers. P(N) is strictly larger than N by Cantor's theorem. But P(N) is also not a subset of N. Condition (iii) is satisfied but adds nothing; and if one considered a case where A is a superset of B (such as the reals containing the naturals), condition (iii) is trivially met. The condition is superfluous.
abductive_insight: The background cases involve either finite sets or sets whose comparison does not raise the subset question, so condition (iii) never becomes relevant. Examination of these cases reveals that the comparison of cardinalities depends solely on the existence or non-existence of injections and bijections between the two sets; the subset relation is a set-theoretic notion orthogonal to cardinality, and its inclusion in the definition is an artefact of the limited range of the background cases.

The third condition might be motivated by the thought that a 'larger' set should not be contained within a 'smaller' one.

Yet one might observe that condition (iii) is both unnecessary and misleading. Consider the comparison between the real numbers and the natural numbers. There is an injection from the naturals to the reals, and Cantor's diagonal argument establishes that no bijection exists. The reals are strictly larger than the naturals. Condition (iii) — that the reals are not a subset of the naturals — is satisfied, but it plays no role in the argument. Now consider the power set of the natural numbers, P(N). Cantor's theorem shows that P(N) is strictly larger than N, and again the subset condition is trivially satisfied but irrelevant. The background cases, by involving either finite sets of equal size or countably infinite sets compared to one another, never brought the subset relation into play in a way that would reveal its irrelevance.
